examples.explanation: Step 1: n=7 > 1, proceed. Step 2: φ(7)=6 (since 7 is prime). Step 3: Test g from 1 to 6. g=1: powers mod 7: 1^1=1, 1^2=1,... only {1}, size 1 ≠ 6, skip. g=2: powers mod 7: 2^1=2, 2^2=4, 2^3=1, 2^4=2, cycle {2,4,1}, size 3 ≠ 6, skip. g=3: powers: 3^1=3, 3^2=2, 3^3=6, 3^4=4, 3^5=5, 3^6=1 → set {1,2,3,4,5,6} size 6 = φ(7), all coprimes covered. Return true.

Step 1: n=8 > 1. Step 2: φ(8)=4 (numbers coprime to 8: 1,3,5,7). Step 3: Test g from 1 to 7. g=1: powers {1}, size 1 ≠ 4. g=3: powers mod 8: 3^1=3, 3^2=1, 3^3=3, 3^4=1 → {1,3}, size 2 ≠ 4. g=5: powers: 5^1=5, 5^2=1, 5^3=5, 5^4=1 → {1,5}, size 2 ≠ 4. g=7: powers: 7^1=7, 7^2=1, 7^3=7, 7^4=1 → {1,7}, size 2 ≠ 4. Others not coprime. No g works. Return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers from 1 to n-1 as candidate primitive roots, checking if each generates all numbers from 1 to n-1 under modulo n. Use modular exponentiation to test each candidate's powers. Suitable for small n but inefficient for larger values.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Primitive Root Existence via Brute Force
ladders.desc: Given modulus n, determine if there exists a primitive root modulo n by testing all candidates from 1 to n-1.
ladders.explanation: A primitive root modulo n is an integer g such that the smallest positive integer k for which g^k ≡ 1 (mod n) is φ(n), where φ is Euler's totient function. This means g generates all numbers from 1 to n-1 that are coprime to n under multiplication modulo n. The brute force approach systematically checks each candidate g from 1 to n-1. For each g, we compute its powers g^1, g^2, ..., g^φ(n) modulo n. If the set of these powers contains all integers from 1 to n-1 that are coprime to n (i.e., all elements of the multiplicative group modulo n), then g is a primitive root. If any g works, return true; otherwise, false. The intuition is that primitive roots exist only for n = 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k where p is an odd prime, but we verify this by direct computation. Step-by-step: 1. If n ≤ 1, return false (no primitive root). 2. Compute φ(n) using Euler's totient. 3. For g from 1 to n-1: a. If gcd(g, n) ≠ 1, skip (not coprime). b. Compute powers g^1 to g^φ(n) mod n. c. Check if these powers generate all numbers from 1 to n-1 coprime to n (size φ(n) and all distinct). d. If yes, return true. 4. Return false. Time: O(n log n) due to n candidates and O(log n) modular exponentiation per power. Space: O(1) excluding input. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts: modular arithmetic, exponentiation, and set generation. Common mistakes: Not checking gcd, incorrect φ(n) computation, or missing the condition that powers must be distinct and cover all coprimes. Real code pattern: Use modular exponentiation via fast powering.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 > 1, proceed. Step 2: φ(7)=6 (since 7 is prime). Step 3: Test g from 1 to 6. g=1: powers mod 7: 1^1=1, 1^2=1,... only {1}, size 1 ≠ 6, skip. g=2: powers mod 7: 2^1=2, 2^2=4, 2^3=1, 2^4=2, cycle {2,4,1}, size 3 ≠ 6, skip. g=3: powers: 3^1=3, 3^2=2, 3^3=6, 3^4=4, 3^5=5, 3^6=1 → set {1,2,3,4,5,6} size 6 = φ(7), all coprimes covered. Return true.

Step 1: n=8 > 1. Step 2: φ(8)=4 (numbers coprime to 8: 1,3,5,7). Step 3: Test g from 1 to 7. g=1: powers {1}, size 1 ≠ 4. g=3: powers mod 8: 3^1=3, 3^2=1, 3^3=3, 3^4=1 → {1,3}, size 2 ≠ 4. g=5: powers: 5^1=5, 5^2=1, 5^3=5, 5^4=1 → {1,5}, size 2 ≠ 4. g=7: powers: 7^1=7, 7^2=1, 7^3=7, 7^4=1 → {1,7}, size 2 ≠ 4. Others not coprime. No g works. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Test if a Number is a Primitive Root Modulo n
ladders.desc: Given candidate g and modulus n, determine if g is a primitive root modulo n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a specific integer g generates the entire multiplicative group modulo n. The multiplicative group consists of integers from 1 to n-1 that are coprime to n, with size φ(n). g is a primitive root if its powers g^1, g^2, ..., g^φ(n) modulo n produce all these coprimes exactly once. The intuition: If g's powers repeat before reaching φ(n) terms, it doesn't generate the full group. Step-by-step: 1. If gcd(g, n) ≠ 1, return false (not in group). 2. Compute φ(n). 3. Initialize an empty set S. 4. For k from 1 to φ(n): a. Compute power = g^k mod n using modular exponentiation. b. If power is in S, return false (duplicate, so cycle shorter). c. Add power to S. 5. After loop, if size of S = φ(n), return true; else false. Time: O(φ(n) log n) for exponentiation per power. Space: O(φ(n)) to store powers. Connection: This is called for each candidate in L0. Common mistakes: Not checking gcd, incorrect φ(n), or off-by-one in loop bounds. Real code pattern: Use fast modular exponentiation and a set for uniqueness check.

examples.trace: Step 1: gcd(2,7)=1, proceed. Step 2: φ(7)=6. Step 3: S={}. k=1: 2^1 mod 7=2, S={2}. k=2: 2^2=4 mod 7=4, S={2,4}. k=3: 2^3=8 mod 7=1, S={1,2,4}. k=4: 2^4=16 mod 7=2, 2 is in S → duplicate, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate Powers of a Number Modulo n
ladders.desc: Compute the sequence g^1, g^2, ..., g^k modulo n for given g, n, and k.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves generating the powers of g under modulo n up to exponent k. The sequence helps determine if g generates a cyclic group. The intuition: By computing successive powers, we can observe patterns and check for cycles. If the powers repeat before reaching k, g does not generate a full cycle of length k. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty list or set for powers. 2. Initialize current = 1. 3. For i from 1 to k: a. Update current = (current * g) mod n (iterative multiplication). b. Record current in the list/set. 4. Return the list/set. Alternatively, use modular exponentiation for each i. Time: O(k) with iterative multiplication, or O(k log n) with exponentiation per step. Space: O(k) to store results. Connection: Used in L1 to get powers for testing primitive root. Common mistakes: Not using modulo at each step (causing overflow), or incorrect loop range. Real code pattern: Use a loop with modular multiplication to build powers efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: powers=[]. current=1. i=1: current = (1*2) mod 7 = 2, powers=[2]. i=2: current = (2*2) mod 7 = 4, powers=[2,4]. i=3: current = (4*2) mod 7 = 8 mod 7 = 1, powers=[2,4,1]. i=4: current = (1*2) mod 7 = 2, powers=[2,4,1,2]. i=5: current = (2*2) mod 7 = 4, powers=[2,4,1,2,4]. i=6: current = (4*2) mod 7 = 1, powers=[2,4,1,2,4,1].

Step 1: powers=[]. current=1. i=1: current = (1*3) mod 7 = 3, powers=[3]. i=2: current = (3*3) mod 7 = 9 mod 7 = 2, powers=[3,2]. i=3: current = (2*3) mod 7 = 6, powers=[3,2,6]. i=4: current = (6*3) mod 7 = 18 mod 7 = 4, powers=[3,2,6,4]. i=5: current = (4*3) mod 7 = 12 mod 7 = 5, powers=[3,2,6,4,5]. i=6: current = (5*3) mod 7 = 15 mod 7 = 1, powers=[3,2,6,4,5,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Exponentiation (Fast Powering)
ladders.desc: Compute g^e mod n efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: Modular exponentiation calculates g^e mod n without computing the large number g^e directly, which is crucial for efficiency. The intuition: Break the exponent e into binary representation and use the property that (a * b) mod n = ((a mod n) * (b mod n)) mod n. By squaring the base repeatedly, we reduce the number of multiplications from O(e) to O(log e). Step-by-step (binary exponentiation): 1. Initialize result = 1, base = g mod n. 2. While e > 0: a. If e is odd, result = (result * base) mod n. b. Base = (base * base) mod n. c. e = e // 2. 3. Return result. Time: O(log e) multiplications modulo n. Space: O(1). Connection: Used in L2 to compute individual powers g^k mod n. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mod at each step, incorrect handling of e=0, or integer overflow in intermediate steps. Real code pattern: Use a while loop with bit checking for odd/even.

examples.trace: Step 1: result=1, base=3 mod 7=3, e=5. e=5 odd: result=(1*3) mod 7=3, base=(3*3) mod 7=9 mod 7=2, e=5//2=2. e=2 even: result=3, base=(2*2) mod 7=4, e=2//2=1. e=1 odd: result=(3*4) mod 7=12 mod 7=5, base=(4*4) mod 7=16 mod 7=2, e=1//2=0. Return result=5.

Step 1: result=1, base=2 mod 11=2, e=10. e=10 even: result=1, base=(2*2) mod 11=4, e=10//2=5. e=5 odd: result=(1*4) mod 11=4, base=(4*4) mod 11=16 mod 11=5, e=5//2=2. e=2 even: result=4, base=(5*5) mod 11=25 mod 11=3, e=2//2=1. e=1 odd: result=(4*3) mod 11=12 mod 11=1, base=(3*3) mod 11=9, e=1//2=0. Return result=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Find the largest positive integer that divides two numbers without remainder.
ladders.explanation: The GCD of two integers a and b is the largest integer d such that d divides both a and b. It's fundamental in number theory, used to check coprimality (gcd=1). The intuition: Use Euclid's algorithm, based on the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This reduces the problem recursively until b=0, then gcd is a. Step-by-step (iterative): 1. While b ≠ 0: a. Set temp = b. b. b = a mod b. c. a = temp. 2. Return a. Time: O(log min(a,b)) due to rapid reduction. Space: O(1). Connection: Used in L1 to check if g and n are coprime. Common mistakes: Not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly. Real code pattern: Use a while loop with modulo operation.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=35, b=14. b≠0: temp=14, b=35 mod 14=7, a=14. b≠0: temp=7, b=14 mod 7=0, a=7. b=0, return a=7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic (Modulo Operation)
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic deals with integers under a modulus n, where we consider numbers equivalent if they differ by a multiple of n. The modulo operation, a mod n, gives the remainder when a is divided by n. Formally, a mod n = r where 0 ≤ r < n and a = qn + r for some integer q. The intuition: It wraps numbers around a circle of size n, useful for cyclic structures like groups. Step-by-step: 1. If n ≤ 0, error (modulus must be positive). 2. Compute quotient q = floor(a / n) (integer division). 3. Compute remainder r = a - q * n. 4. Adjust r to be non-negative (if a negative, r might be negative; add n until 0 ≤ r < n). Time: O(1) for basic arithmetic. Space: O(1). Connection: Used in all higher levels for operations modulo n. Common mistakes: Confusing modulo with remainder (different for negative numbers), or using n=0. Real code pattern: In programming, use % operator, but handle negatives by adding n if result < 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 > 0. Step 2: q = floor(-3/7) = -1 (since integer division rounds down). Step 3: r = -3 - (-1)*7 = -3 + 7 = 4. Step 4: r=4, 0 ≤ 4 < 7, return 4.

approaches.name: Factorization and Euler Totient O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Use the theorem that a primitive root exists modulo n if and only if n is 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k for an odd prime p. Factorize n to check this condition and compute Euler's totient function φ(n) to determine the number of primitive roots. Relies on prime factorization and mathematical properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Primitive Root Existence Modulo n
ladders.desc: Given an integer n > 0, determine if a primitive root exists modulo n using factorization and Euler's totient theorem.
ladders.explanation: A primitive root modulo n is an integer g such that every number coprime to n is congruent to a power of g modulo n. The theorem states: primitive roots exist modulo n if and only if n is 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k, where p is an odd prime and k ≥ 1. This approach leverages this theorem by factorizing n to check these conditions. First, handle base cases: if n is 1, return false (no modulus); if n is 2 or 4, return true. For other n, factorize it to extract prime factors and their exponents. If n has more than two distinct prime factors, return false. If n has exactly one distinct prime factor p, check: if p is 2, then n must be 2^k; only 2 and 4 work, so return false for other powers of 2. If p is odd, then n = p^k works for any k ≥ 1, so return true. If n has two distinct prime factors, they must be 2 and an odd prime p, with n = 2 * p^k (since 2p^k includes cases where k=1, i.e., 2p). Verify that the exponent of 2 is exactly 1 (i.e., n is not divisible by 4) and the odd prime has exponent k ≥ 1. If all conditions hold, return true; otherwise, false. Time complexity is O(√n) due to factorization, and space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the factorization subroutine to analyze n's structure. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle n=1, misapplying the theorem for powers of 2 beyond 4, or not checking the exponent of 2 in the two-prime case. Real code pattern: def primitive_root_exists(n): if n <= 0: return False; if n == 2 or n == 4: return True; factors = factorize(n); distinct_primes = list(factors.keys()); if len(distinct_primes) > 2: return False; if len(distinct_primes) == 1: p = distinct_primes[0]; if p == 2: return False; else: return True; if len(distinct_primes) == 2: if 2 not in factors: return False; if factors[2] != 1: return False; other_prime = [p for p in distinct_primes if p != 2][0]; if other_prime % 2 == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 7 > 0. Step 2: n is not 2 or 4. Step 3: Factorize 7: prime factors = {7: 1}. Step 4: Distinct primes = [7], length = 1. Step 5: Single prime case: p = 7, which is odd. Step 6: Return true because 7 is an odd prime (p^k with k=1).

Step 1: n = 12 > 0. Step 2: n is not 2 or 4. Step 3: Factorize 12: prime factors = {2: 2, 3: 1}. Step 4: Distinct primes = [2, 3], length = 2. Step 5: Two prime case: check if 2 is present: yes. Step 6: Check exponent of 2: factors[2] = 2, which is not 1. Step 7: Return false because n is divisible by 4 (2^2), so not of form 2p^k.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization of n
ladders.desc: Factorize an integer n into its prime factors and their exponents.
ladders.explanation: Prime factorization breaks n into a product of prime numbers, recording each prime's exponent. This is crucial for checking the primitive root existence conditions, as we need to analyze n's structure (e.g., number of distinct primes, their values, and exponents). Intuition: Every integer > 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes. We iterate from 2 to √n, dividing n by each prime factor as found. Step-by-step: Initialize an empty dictionary factors. While n is even, divide n by 2 and increment factors[2]. Then, for i from 3 to √n, step 2 (checking odd numbers), while n is divisible by i, divide n by i and increment factors[i]. After the loop, if n > 1, it's a prime factor, so add to factors. Time complexity: O(√n) in worst case (when n is prime), as we check up to √n. Space: O(1) for storage, but output size depends on number of prime factors. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the factorization needed to apply the theorem. Common mistakes: not handling n=1 (return empty), forgetting to add the last prime factor if n > 1, or inefficiently checking all numbers instead of odds after 2. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): factors = {}; if n == 1: return factors; while n % 2 == 0: factors[2] = factors.get(2, 0) + 1; n //= 2; i = 3; while i * i <= n: while n % i == 0: factors[i] = factors.get(i, 0) + 1; n //= i; i += 2; if n > 1: factors[n] = factors.get(n, 0) + 1; return factors.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 18. Step 2: Check even: 18 % 2 == 0, so factors[2] = 1, n = 9. Step 3: i = 3, 3*3=9 <= 9: 9 % 3 == 0, factors[3] = 1, n = 3; again 3 % 3 == 0, factors[3] = 2, n = 1. Step 4: i = 5, 5*5=25 > 1, stop. Step 5: n = 1, so done. Output: {2: 1, 3: 2}.
Step 1: n = 7. Step 2: Check even: 7 % 2 != 0. Step 3: i = 3, 3*3=9 > 7, stop loop. Step 4: n = 7 > 1, so factors[7] = 1. Output: {7: 1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Apply Primitive Root Existence Theorem
ladders.desc: Given prime factorization of n, determine if n satisfies the conditions for primitive root existence.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the logical application of the theorem without performing factorization. Input is the factorization (a dictionary of prime: exponent), and output is a boolean. Intuition: The theorem reduces the check to simple conditions on prime factors. Steps: If n is 1, return false. If n is 2 or 4, return true (handled separately, but here assume factorization given). Extract distinct primes and their counts. If count > 2, return false. If count == 1: let p be the prime. If p == 2, return false (since only 2 and 4 work, and 4 has exponent 2, but here if p=2 and exponent>2, it's invalid). Actually, for a single prime p=2, only exponents 1 and 2 work (n=2 or 4), but since factorization is input, we check: if p=2 and exponent==1 or 2, return true? Wait, in full problem, we handle n=2 and 4 earlier; here, assume we check: if p=2, return false for any exponent (since only 2 and 4 are valid, but 4 has exponent 2, so we need to handle). To simplify: if single prime and p is odd, return true; if p=2, return false (because beyond 2 and 4, no primitive roots). If count == 2: check if one prime is 2 and its exponent is exactly 1, and the other prime is odd. If so, return true; else false. Time: O(k) where k is number of distinct primes, but k ≤ 2, so O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses factorization output to make the decision. Common mistakes: misinterpreting the theorem for powers of 2, or not verifying the exponent of 2 in two-prime case. Real code pattern: def check_condition(factors): distinct = list(factors.keys()); if len(distinct) > 2: return False; if len(distinct) == 1: p = distinct[0]; if p == 2: return False; else: return True; if len(distinct) == 2: if 2 not in factors: return False; if factors[2] != 1: return False; other = [p for p in distinct if p != 2][0]; if other % 2 == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Distinct primes = [7], count = 1. Step 2: Single prime case: p = 7, which is odd. Step 3: Return true.
Step 1: Distinct primes = [2, 3], count = 2. Step 2: Two prime case: check if 2 is present: yes. Step 3: Check exponent of 2: factors[2] = 2, which is not 1. Step 4: Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Identify Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Check if a given integer is prime.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is an integer greater than 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. This is a building block because in factorization, we need to identify primes, and in the theorem, we check for odd primes. Intuition: To check if n is prime, test divisibility by integers from 2 to √n. If any divides n, n is composite; otherwise, prime. Step-by-step: If n <= 1, return false. If n == 2, return true. If n % 2 == 0, return false. For i from 3 to √n, step 2 (check odd divisors), if n % i == 0, return false. If no divisors found, return true. Time complexity: O(√n) for worst case (when n is prime). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This helps in verifying if a factor is an odd prime in the theorem application. Common mistakes: not handling n=2 separately, checking up to n-1 instead of √n, or not optimizing by checking only odd numbers after 2. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n <= 1: return False; if n == 2: return True; if n % 2 == 0: return False; i = 3; while i * i <= n: if n % i == 0: return False; i += 2; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 7 > 1. Step 2: n != 2. Step 3: 7 % 2 != 0. Step 4: i = 3, 3*3=9 > 7, so stop. Step 5: No divisors found, return true.
Step 1: n = 9 > 1. Step 2: n != 2. Step 3: 9 % 2 != 0. Step 4: i = 3, 3*3=9 <= 9, and 9 % 3 == 0, so return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Integer Square Root
ladders.desc: Find the largest integer ≤ √n for a given non-negative integer n.
ladders.explanation: The integer square root, often used in prime checking and factorization loops, is the floor of the square root. Intuition: We need to iterate up to √n, so computing this efficiently avoids floating-point errors. Step-by-step: For n < 2, return n. Use a binary search or simple iterative method. Here, a simple method: initialize low = 0, high = n. While low ≤ high, mid = (low + high) // 2. If mid*mid == n, return mid. If mid*mid < n, low = mid + 1, ans = mid. Else, high = mid - 1. Return ans. Time complexity: O(log n) with binary search. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in is_prime to limit the loop to √n. Common mistakes: using math.sqrt which may introduce floating-point inaccuracies, or not handling edge cases like n=0 or 1. Real code pattern: def int_sqrt(n): if n < 2: return n; low, high = 0, n; ans = 0; while low <= high: mid = (low + high) // 2; if mid * mid == n: return mid; elif mid * mid < n: low = mid + 1; ans = mid; else: high = mid - 1; return ans.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 16 >= 2. Step 2: low=0, high=16. Step 3: mid=8, 8*8=64 > 16, high=7. Step 4: mid=3, 3*3=9 < 16, low=4, ans=3. Step 5: mid=5, 5*5=25 > 16, high=4. Step 6: mid=4, 4*4=16 == 16, return 4.

Step 1: n = 10 >= 2. Step 2: low=0, high=10. Step 3: mid=5, 5*5=25 > 10, high=4. Step 4: mid=2, 2*2=4 < 10, low=3, ans=2. Step 5: mid=3, 3*3=9 < 10, low=4, ans=3. Step 6: mid=4, 4*4=16 > 10, high=3. Step 7: low=4 > high=3, stop, return ans=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Divisibility and Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Check if one integer divides another using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: Divisibility is a fundamental concept in number theory. An integer a divides b if b % a == 0, meaning there is no remainder when b is divided by a. The modulo operator % returns the remainder of division. Intuition: This is used throughout the algorithm to test factors, check primes, and handle conditions. Step-by-step: Given integers a and b, to check if a divides b, compute b % a. If result is 0, then a divides b; otherwise, it does not. Time complexity: O(1) for the operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This operation is used in computing integer square root (e.g., in comparisons) and in higher levels for factorization. Common mistakes: confusing % with division, or not handling a=0 (division by zero). Real code pattern: def divides(a, b): if a == 0: return False; return b % a == 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: a = 3, b = 9. Step 2: Compute 9 % 3 = 0. Step 3: Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 9, return true.
Step 1: a = 4, b = 10. Step 2: Compute 10 % 4 = 2. Step 3: Since remainder is 2 (not 0), 4 does not divide 10, return false.

approaches.name: Pollard Rho and Advanced Factorization O(n^(1/4))
approaches.summary: Employ advanced factorization algorithms like Pollard Rho to efficiently factorize n, then apply the primitive root existence theorem. This is optimal for large n where sqrt(n) factorization is too slow. Combines probabilistic factorization with the same theoretical check as the factorization approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Primitive Root Existence Check Using Pollard Rho Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete solution to check if a primitive root exists modulo n using Pollard Rho factorization and the primitive root theorem.
ladders.explanation: This solution determines whether a primitive root exists modulo n by combining advanced factorization with number theory. The intuition is that primitive roots exist only for specific moduli: 2, 4, p^k, and 2p^k where p is an odd prime. For large n, naive factorization up to √n is too slow (O(√n)), so we use Pollard Rho algorithm which runs in O(n^(1/4)) expected time to factor n efficiently. The algorithm first handles small cases directly: n <= 1 returns false, and n=2 or n=4 return true. For n>4, we factor n using Pollard Rho to get its prime factorization. Then we check the conditions: if n has any prime factor other than 2 or an odd prime, or if n is divisible by 8, then no primitive root exists. Otherwise, we check if n is of the form p^k or 2p^k where p is an odd prime. The Pollard Rho algorithm uses Floyd's cycle detection with a polynomial function f(x) = (x² + c) mod n to find a non-trivial factor. The space complexity is O(1) as we only store a few variables. Connection to L1: This level integrates the Pollard Rho factorization (L1) with the primitive root theorem check. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle n<=1,2,4 boundary cases, incorrect cycle detection in Pollard Rho, and not checking all conditions of the primitive root theorem. Real code pattern: def primitive_root_exists(n): if n <= 1: return False; if n in [2,4]: return True; factors = pollard_rho_factorize(n); if any(p not in [2] and p%2==0 for p in factors): return False; if n%8==0: return False; check if n is p^k or 2p^k; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 > 4, not a special case. Step 2: Factorize 7 using Pollard Rho: Start with x=2, c=1, y=2. Iteration 1: x = (2²+1)%7=5, y = ((5²+1)%7)²+1)%7 = (26%7=5)²+1=26%7=5, gcd(|x-y|,7)=gcd(0,7)=7, no factor found. Try c=2: x=2,y=2. x=6,y=((6²+2)%7=38%7=3)²+2=11%7=4, gcd(|6-4|,7)=gcd(2,7)=1. Continue until factor found: 7 is prime, factors=[7]. Step 3: Check conditions: factors only contain odd prime 7, n=7 is 7¹ (p^k form). Step 4: Return True.

Step 1: n=15 > 4. Step 2: Factorize 15: Pollard Rho with x=2,c=1: x=5,y=((5²+1)%15=26%15=11)²+1=122%15=2, gcd(|5-2|,15)=gcd(3,15)=3, factor found. Factors: 3 and 5. Step 3: Check conditions: factors contain two different odd primes (3 and 5), not of form p^k or 2p^k. Step 4: Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard Rho Factorization Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Pollard Rho algorithm to find a non-trivial factor of n.
ladders.explanation: Pollard Rho is a probabilistic algorithm to find a non-trivial factor of a composite number n in O(n^(1/4)) expected time. The intuition is based on the birthday paradox and cycle detection in a pseudo-random sequence. We define a function f(x) = (x² + c) mod n, where c is a constant. Starting from a random x, we generate the sequence x, f(x), f(f(x)), ... Due to the pigeonhole principle, this sequence will eventually repeat modulo a factor d of n, creating a cycle. We use Floyd's cycle detection algorithm with two pointers (slow and fast) to detect this cycle efficiently. When we find x and y such that gcd(|x-y|, n) > 1, we have found a factor. The algorithm is recursive: once a factor is found, we recursively factorize the factor and n/factor. The time complexity is O(n^(1/4)) because the cycle length is expected to be O(√p) where p is the smallest prime factor, and √p ≤ n^(1/4) for balanced factors. Space is O(1) for iterative implementation. Connection to L0: This factorization is used in L0 to get prime factors of n for the primitive root check. Common mistakes include choosing c=0 or c=-2 which can lead to trivial cycles, not using absolute value in gcd, and infinite recursion for prime numbers. Real code pattern: def pollard_rho(n): if n%2==0: return 2; x=2; y=2; c=1; d=1; while d==1: x=(x*x+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; d=math.gcd(abs(x-y),n); if d==n: return pollard_rho(n) with different c; return d.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=15 odd, not divisible by 2. Step 2: Initialize x=2, y=2, c=1, d=1. Step 3: Iteration 1: x=(4+1)%15=5, y=(4+1)%15=5, y=(25+1)%15=26%15=11, d=gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3. Step 4: d=3 !=1 and !=15, return 3.
Step 1: n=49 odd. Step 2: x=2,y=2,c=1,d=1. Step 3: Iteration 1: x=5,y=5,y=26%49=26,d=gcd(|5-26|,49)=gcd(21,49)=7. Step 4: d=7, return 7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Primitive Root Existence Theorem Conditions
ladders.desc: Apply the theorem to determine if a primitive root exists based on prime factorization.
ladders.explanation: The primitive root existence theorem states that a primitive root modulo n exists if and only if n is 1, 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k, where p is an odd prime and k ≥ 1. The intuition is that the multiplicative group modulo n must be cyclic, which only happens for these forms. For n>4, we first factor n into primes. If n has any prime factor other than 2 or an odd prime (e.g., composite factors), no primitive root. If n is divisible by 8 (i.e., has 2³ as factor), no primitive root because the group modulo 2^k for k≥3 is not cyclic. Then, we check the structure: if n is a power of an odd prime (p^k), yes; if n is 2 times a power of an odd prime (2p^k), yes; otherwise, no. The algorithm: factor n; if factors contain any even prime other than 2 or any prime with exponent >1 not in allowed forms, return false; check n%8==0; then check p^k or 2p^k. Time O(f) where f is number of factors, space O(1). Connection to L0: This is the decision logic applied after factorization in L0. Common mistakes: misapplying the theorem for n=2p^k when p=2, not handling exponents correctly, and confusing with Euler's totient function conditions. Real code pattern: def check_conditions(factors): if any(p!=2 and p%2==0 for p in factors): return False; if n%8==0: return False; if len(set(factors))==1 and factors[0]%2==1: return True; if len(factors)==2 and 2 in factors and other is odd prime power: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: factors only contain odd prime 7. Step 2: n=7 not divisible by 8. Step 3: len(set)=1 and prime is odd, so n=7¹ (p^k form). Step 4: Return True.
Step 1: factors contain 2 and odd prime 7. Step 2: n=14 not divisible by 8. Step 3: len(factors)=2, 2 is present, other factor 7 is odd prime power (7¹). Step 4: n=2*7¹ (2p^k form). Return True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Computation
ladders.desc: Compute gcd of two numbers using Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (gcd) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). The intuition is that any common divisor of a and b also divides their difference and remainder, so we can reduce the problem recursively. Algorithm: while b != 0: set temp = b; b = a mod b; a = temp; return a. For example, gcd(48,18): 48 mod 18 = 12, then gcd(18,12): 18 mod 12 = 6, then gcd(12,6): 12 mod 6 = 0, so gcd=6. Time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) as the numbers reduce quickly. Space is O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L1: GCD is used in Pollard Rho to find factors by computing gcd(|x-y|, n). Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (use absolute values), infinite loop if b=0 initially, and confusion with modular arithmetic. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: b=18 !=0, compute a mod b = 48 mod 18 = 12. Set a=18, b=12. Step 2: b=12 !=0, compute 18 mod 12 = 6. Set a=12, b=6. Step 3: b=6 !=0, compute 12 mod 6 = 0. Set a=6, b=0. Step 4: b=0, return a=6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic and Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Perform modular operations including addition, multiplication, and exponentiation.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic deals with numbers wrapping around after reaching a modulus n. For integers a and b, a mod n is the remainder when a is divided by n. Basic operations: (a + b) mod n = (a mod n + b mod n) mod n; (a * b) mod n = (a mod n * b mod n) mod n. Modular exponentiation computes a^b mod n efficiently using exponentiation by squaring to avoid large intermediate numbers. Intuition: break exponent b into binary, e.g., a^13 = a^8 * a^4 * a^1. Algorithm: result = 1; while b > 0: if b is odd, result = (result * a) mod n; a = (a * a) mod n; b = b // 2; return result. This reduces time from O(b) to O(log b). Space O(1). Connection to L3: Modular operations are used in GCD (via mod) and in Pollard Rho function f(x). Common mistakes: forgetting to mod at each step leading to overflow, incorrect handling of negative mods, and confusion with modular inverse. Real code pattern: def mod_pow(a,b,n): res=1; a%=n; while b>0: if b&1: res=(res*a)%n; a=(a*a)%n; b>>=1; return res.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factorization
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers and basic factorization by trial division.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2,3,5,7 are primes. Factorization is the process of breaking a number into its prime factors, e.g., 12 = 2² * 3. The intuition is that every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely represented as a product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Basic factorization by trial division: for a number n, test divisibility by integers from 2 up to √n. If n is divisible by d, add d to factors and divide n by d; otherwise increment d. Algorithm: factors=[]; d=2; while d*d <= n: while n%d==0: factors.append(d); n//=d; d+=1; if n>1: factors.append(n). Time O(√n) in worst case (when n is prime). Space O(log n) for storing factors. Connection to L4: Factorization uses modular arithmetic (division check) and is a simpler version of Pollard Rho in L1. Common mistakes: not handling n=1 correctly, inefficient by checking all numbers instead of just primes, and missing the final prime factor. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): factors=[]; d=2; while d*d<=n: while n%d==0: factors.append(d); n//=d; d+=1; if n>1: factors.append(n); return factors.
